title: Apple sector veteran honoured for 'extraordinary contribution'
description_clean: For over 50 years Mel Ewers has been supporting the apple sector navigate through compliance and quality assurance issues. And Ewers’ contribution was recognised last night with the New Zealand Apples & Pears Incorporated (NZAPI) Outstanding Contribution Award at dinner in Queenstown. Around 260 people, who attended the function, heard that Ewers has quietly made an extraordinary contribution to the sector, supporting growers, packhouses, exporters, and industry organisations through her expertise in compliance, logistics, quality assurance, market access, training, and biosecurity. summary: Mel Ewers received the New Zealand Apples & Pears Incorporated Outstanding Contribution Award in Queenstown for over 50 years of supporting the apple sector in compliance, logistics, quality assurance, market access, training and biosecurity.

title: Minister praises apple sector’s growth story
description_clean: The apple sector is enjoying a bumper harvest season. Exports so far this season sit at 17.3 million tray carton equivalents, up 2 million on last year and 2.5 million on pre-cyclone levels. Addressing NZ Apple and Pear Conference in Queenstown today, Trade and Agriculture Minister Todd McClay noted that Royal Gala variety was in hot demand at 93% shipped. He told 300 growers and stakeholders that their growth story speaks for itself. Exports have risen from $347 million in 2012 to $1.1 billion by November last year. This year's Situation and Outlook forecasts apple and pear exports up 6% to $
summary: Trade and Agriculture Minister Todd McClay praised the apple sector's growth at the NZ Apple and Pear Conference, noting exports of 17.3 million tray carton equivalents this season and a rise from $347 million in 2012 to $1.1 billion by November last year.

title: Campaign Urges Growers to Plan Ahead on Resistance
description_clean: Growers and farmers are being encouraged to have an important conversation before the coming season starts: how to prolong the effectiveness of the crop protection tools they rely on. Keep What Works Working is an industry-wide campaign led by the A Lighter Touch programme. It aims to raise awareness of resistance management and encourage growers to discuss their plans with crop protection advisers before spray programmes are finalised. The resistance management can have a significant impact on how long valuable agrichemical tools remain effective. Growers are already experiencing the impact o
summary: The 'Keep What Works Working' campaign, led by the A Lighter Touch programme, urges growers to discuss resistance management with crop protection advisers before finalising spray programmes.

title: Ballance Launches AI Assistant for Fertiliser Ordering
description_clean: A new digital assistant is now helping Ballance Agri-Nutrients farmers speed up nutrient information and ordering. The new digital assistance in the MyBallance app helps users quickly access key information about their nutrient recommendations, product and pricing information and place fertiliser orders directly from their phone. Ballance Assistant was developed using AI technology alongside direct input from farmers. Ballance Agri-Nutrients digital transformation lead Danusia Bathgate says farmers have made it clear they don't want to be in their office or on their computer working with compl
summary: Ballance Agri-Nutrients has launched an AI-developed digital assistant in its MyBallance app to help farmers access nutrient information and place fertiliser orders from their phones.

title: Apple, pear sector stakeholders meet for annual conference
description_clean: New Zealand apple and pear industry stakeholders are out in force for the annual conference in Queenstown. Growers, exporters, researchers, government representatives and industry partners alike have gathered to discuss the challenges and opportunities that are shaping the sector’s future. The first day of the two-day conference yesterday featured a packed programme of speakers and panel discussions covering topics including research and development, innovation, biosecurity, and what’s likely in the future for New Zealand’s apple and pear growers. NZAPI’s annual general meeting was also held o
summary: New Zealand apple and pear industry stakeholders gathered in Queenstown for a two-day annual conference covering R&D, innovation, biosecurity and the sector's future, with NZAPI's AGM also held.

title: The Banker Who Still Farms: Cameron Reed's Two Worlds
description_clean: Cameron Reed lives between two worlds. One day he might be in a boardroom, making calls on the agri strategy for New Zealand’s largest bank across the lower half of the South Island. The next, he’s back on the family farm in Darfield, putting up a break fence while reflecting on the same pressures his customers are facing. Reed is ANZ’s provincial southern head of business and agri, overseeing the bank’s business and farm lending across the lower South Island, while also carrying responsibility for helping to shape ANZ’s agri strategy and direction. His family has been farming in Darfield for
summary: ANZ's provincial southern head of business and agri, Cameron Reed, balances his banking role overseeing lending across the lower South Island with farming on his family's Darfield property.

title: Global Shocks Are No Longer Distant
description_clean: OPINION: At Fieldays this year, one theme kept surfacing across the sector: global shocks are no longer something that happens “over there”. They are now translating quickly into local, farm-level impacts through increasingly complex and connected channels. New Zealand’s agribusiness sector has long been defined by its ability to adapt. Droughts, floods, biosecurity events and global market cycles have made resilience a core part of how the sector operates. That resilience is not accidental. It is the product of decades of structural change and a culture of adaptation. What has emerged is a se
summary: An opinion piece from Fieldays argues that global shocks now translate quickly into farm-level impacts in New Zealand, and highlights the agribusiness sector's culture of resilience and adaptation.
